title: Hands-0n Exercise 2:
text:
In this chapter, I learned about ggplot2 extensions for creating more effective statistical graphics.
The goals of this exercise was to:

control the placement of annotation on a graph by using functions provided in ggrepel package,
create professional publication quality figure by using functions provided in ggthemes and hrbrthemes packages, and
plot composite figure by combining ggplot2 graphs by using patchwork package.

title: Hands-0n Exercise 2:
text:
3.1 Working with ggrepel


Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")

title: Hands-0n Exercise 2:
text:
5.1 Creating Composite Graphics: pathwork methods
There are several ggplot2 extension’s functions support the needs to prepare composite figure by combining several graphs such as grid.arrange() of gridExtra package and plot_grid() of cowplot package. In this section, we use ggplot2 extension called patchwork which is specially designed for combining separate ggplot2 graphs into a single figure.
Patchwork package has a very simple syntax, such as:

Two-Column Layout using the Plus Sign +.
Parenthesis () to create a subplot group.
Two-Row Layout using the Division Sign /

title: Hands-0n Exercise 2:
text:
5.2 Combining two ggplot2 graphs
Figure below shows a composite of two histograms created using patchwork.

HorizontalVerticalRelative area




Show the code
p1 + p2













Show the code
p1 / p2













Show the code
p1 + p2 + plot_layout(ncol=2,widths=c(2,1))

title: Hands-0n Exercise 2:
text:
5.3 Combining three ggplot2 graphs
We can plot more complex composite by using appropriate operators. For example, the composite figure below is plotted by using:

“|” operator to stack two ggplot2 graphs,
“/” operator to place the plots beside each other,
“()” operator the define the sequence of the plotting.


/ and | operators- operator (subtraction)Nested layoutsWith non-ggplot content




Show the code
(p1 / p2) | p3













Show the code
p1 + p2 - p3 + plot_layout(ncol=1)













Show the code
p3 + {
  p1 + p2 + plot_layout(ncol=1)
}













Show the code
((p1 / p2) | p3) + grid:: textGrob('(Can add content here.)', hjust=0, x=-0, gp=grid::gpar(font=3, fontsize = 12))












To learn more, refer to Plot Assembly.

title: Hands-0n Exercise 2:
section: 5.4 Creating a composite figure with tag
text:
5.4 Creating a composite figure with tag
In order to identify subplots in text, patchwork also provides auto-tagging capabilities as shown in the figure below.

EnumerationWith tagging




Show the code
((p1 / p2) | p3) +
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')













Show the code
((p1 / p2) | p3)  + 
  plot_layout(tag_level = 'new') +
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = c('A', '1'), tag_prefix = 'Fig. ', tag_sep = '.', 
                  tag_suffix = ':')

title: Hands-0n Exercise 2:
text:
5.5 Creating figure with inset
Beside providing functions to place plots next to each other based on the provided layout. With inset_element() of patchwork, we can place one or several plots or graphic elements freely on top or below another plot.


Show the code
p4 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Math scores")

p5 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")

p6 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Correlation between English & Math scores")

p6 +  inset_element(p5,
                    left = 0.02,
                    bottom=0.7,
                    right= 0.5,
                    top=1)

title: Hands-0n Exercise 2:
text:
5.6 Composite figure: Patchwork + ggtheme
Figure below is created by combining patchwork and theme_economist() of ggthemes package discussed earlier.

theme_economist()theme_solarized




Show the code
patchwork <- ((p4/p5) | p6)

patchwork & theme_economist() +
  theme(plot.title=element_text(size =10),
                                        axis.title.y=element_text(size = 9,
                                                                  angle = 0,
                                                                  vjust=0.9),
                                         axis.title.x=element_text(size = 9))













Show the code
patchwork & theme_solarized() +
  theme(plot.title=element_text(size =10),
                                        axis.title.y=element_text(size = 9,
                                                                  angle = 0,
                                                                  vjust=0.9),
                                         axis.title.x=element_text(size = 9))

title: About
text:
About this site

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
Basic principles and essential components of GGPLOT2.
Deep dive on each of the “Grammar of Graphics” building blocks.
Apply essential graphical elements provided by GGPLOT2 to create elegant and functional statistical graphics.

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
A layered grammar of graphics
Figure below shows the seven grammars of GGPLOT2.


Themes: modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.
Coordinates: define the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Statistics: Statistical transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Facets: split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Geometries: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
Aesthetics: Take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colors, size, shape, or transparency.
Data: The dataset being plotted.

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
GGPLOT2 introduction
GGPLOT2 is an R package for creating data-driven graphics based on the “Grammar of Graphics”. It is also part of the tidyverse family specially designed for visual exploration and communication. For more detail, visit ggplot2 link.

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
R Graphics VS GGPLOT2
Comparison between R graphics and GGPLOT2

R GraphicsGGPLOT2




Show the code
hist(exam_data$MATHS, 
     ylab='Number of Students', 
     xlab='Score', 
     main='Distribution of Maths scores',
     col='#FFC107')













Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="black", 
                 fill="#FFC107") +
  labs(x = "Score",
       y = "Number of Students",
       title = "Distribution of Maths scores")













Why is GGPLOT2 recommended?

The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive. ~ Hadley Wickham

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
6.1 BASIC GEOMETRIES






NoteObject: geom_bar



The code chunk below plots a bar chart by using geom_bar().




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()















NoteObject: geom_dotplot



In a dot plot, the width of a dot corresponds to the bin width (or maximum width, depending on the binning algorithm), and dots are stacked, with each dot representing one observation.



geom_dotplot()geom_dotplot() - modified








Caution




The y scale is not very useful, in fact it is very misleading.
binwidth defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data.





Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)

















NoteFix



The code chunk below performs the following two steps:

scale_y_continuous() is used to turn off the y-axis, and - binwidth argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5.





Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)  


















NoteObject: geom_histogram



In the code chunk below, geom_histogram() is used to create a simple histogram by using values in MATHS field of exam_data.



geom_histogram()geom_histogram() - modified








Caution



Note that the default bin is 30.



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       
















NoteFix



For grades, a binwidth of 5 or 10 is the standard “human logic” approach.



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)       

















NoteObject: geom_point



geom_point() is especially useful for creating scatterplot.



geom_point()geom_point() - modified








NoteConfiguration



The code chunk below plots a scatterplot showing the Maths and English grades of pupils by using geom_point(), grouped by gender.



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= ENGLISH,
           color = GENDER)) +
  geom_point()
















NoteConfiguration



To add reference lines:



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= ENGLISH, 
           y = MATHS, 
           color = GENDER)) +
  geom_point() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 50),
             col = 'grey',
             linewidth = 0.5,
             linetype = "dashed") + 
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 50),
             col = 'grey',
             linewidth = 0.5,
             linetype = "dashed") +  
  labs(x = "English Score",
       y = "Math Score",
       title = "Math Score against English Score by Gender")

















NoteObject: geom_boxplot



geom_boxplot() displays continuous value list. It visualises five summary statistics (the median, two hinges and two whiskers), and all “outlying” points individually.

originalnotchesfacet_wrap



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()
















NoteConfiguration



Notches are used in box plots to help visually assess whether the medians of distributions differ. If the notches do not overlap, this is evidence that the medians are different.
The code chunk below plots the distribution of Maths scores by gender in notched plot instead of boxplot.



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)
















NoteConfiguration



We can also incorporate facet_wrap() to create boxplots of math scores for each class that is separated by gender.



ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x = CLASS)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  facet_wrap(~ GENDER)

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
6.2 ADVANCE GEOMETRIES






NoteObject: geom_hex




Usage: It divides the plot into regular hexagons and colors each hexagon based on the number of points (density) within it.
Best for: Visualizing the relationship between two continuous variables when you have a large dataset (thousands of points).





Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = CLASS)) + geom_hex()















NoteObject: geom_density




Usage: Use this when you want to see the underlying shape of the data without the jagged edges of histogram bins.
Best for: Visualizing the distribution of a single continuous variable.





Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) + geom_density()















NoteObject: geom_violin




Usage: It is essentially a mirrored version of a density plot, rotated 90 degrees and displayed like a boxplot.
Best for: Comparing the distribution of a continuous variable across different categories (one discrete, one continuous).





Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = GENDER)) + geom_violin()

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
GEOMETRIC MODIFICATIONS

with geom()with aes()








NoteConfiguration




bins argument is used to change the number of bins,
fill argument is used to shade the histogram, and
color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars





Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="#FFC107")  

















NoteConfiguration




The code chunk below changes the interior color of the histogram (i.e. fill) by using sub-group of aesthetic().
This approach can be used to color or fill the alpha of the geometric.





Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")


















NoteCOMBINATION OF OBJECTS



The code chunks below plots the data points on the boxplots by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point().






Important



The order of geom layer matters! Check code chunks below.





CombinationCombination - Modified








NoteConfiguration



geom_point() before geom_boxplot()



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +

  geom_point(position = 'jitter',
             size=0.5) +
  geom_boxplot()
















NoteConfiguration



geom_boxplot() before geom_point()



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
7.1 FUNCTION: FACET_WRAP()






NoteOverview



facet_wrap wraps a 1d sequence of panels into 2d. This is generally a better use of screen space than facet_grid because most displays are roughly rectangular.



Subsetting dataMultiple overlay of HistogramsGrouped data








NoteConfiguration



If we only want to visualise classes 3A, 3B, 3C, 3D, we could subset the exam data to include only these students using filter() from the dplyr package.




Show the code
sub_data <- exam_data %>% 
  filter(CLASS %in% c('3A', '3B', '3C', '3D'))

ggplot(data=sub_data,
       aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
  facet_wrap(~CLASS) +
  labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Math scores',
       title = "Maths scores for Classes 3A, 3B, 3C, 3D")

















NoteConfiguration



We could also overlay the genders if we want to.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS, fill = GENDER)) + #Add fill to split histogram categories
  geom_histogram(position = "identity", alpha=0.2, bins=20) +
  labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Math scores',
       title = "Maths scores by Gender")

















NoteConfiguration



Two separate graphs but using the same grouped data.




Show the code
ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x = CLASS)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  facet_wrap(~ GENDER)

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
7.2 FUNCTION: FACET_GRID()






NoteOverview



facet_grid() forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data.








NoteConfiguration



The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using facet_grid().




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
  facet_grid(~CLASS) +
  labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Math scores',
       title = "Maths scores by Class")

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
8.1 Using STAT Function

stat()stat_summary()








NoteConfiguration



The boxplots below are incomplete because the positions of the means were not shown.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes( y= MATHS, x = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()

















NoteConfiguration



The code chunk below adds mean values by using stat_summary() function and overriding the default geom.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = 'point',
               fun.y='mean',
               colour = 'red',
               size=4)

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
8.2 Using GEOM Function

geom()geom_smooth()








NoteConfiguration



The code chunk below adding mean values by using geom_() function and overriding the default stat.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun.y="mean",           
             colour ="red",          
             size=4)

















NoteConfiguration



In the code chunk below, geom_smooth() is used to plot a best fit curve on the scatterplot.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=1)

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
8.3 Adding a best fit curve






NoteOverview



The default method used in geom_smooth()is loess.








NoteConfiguration









Note



The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.






Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=1, method = lm)

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
9.1 coord_flip()

vertical (default)horizontal








NoteConfiguration



Bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form by default.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()+
    labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Race',
       title = "Number of Students by Race")

















NoteConfiguration



The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip().




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
    labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Race',
       title = "Number of Students by Race")

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
9.2 coord_cartesian

defaultedited








NoteConfiguration



The scatterplot below is slightly misleading because the y-aixs and x-axis range are not equal.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +
      labs(y='English', 
       x='Maths',
       title = "Graph of English against Math scores")

















NoteConfiguration



The code chunk below fixed both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +
  coord_cartesian (xlim=c(0,100),
                   ylim= c(0,100))+
        labs(y='English', 
       x='Maths',
       title = "Graph of English against Math scores")

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
10.1 Built-in themes

theme_minimal()theme_classic()








NoteConfiguration



A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_minimal().




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

















NoteConfiguration



A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_classic().




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()

title: Hands-0n Exercise 1:
text:
10.2 Special built-in Themes

theme_void()theme_test()








NoteConfiguration



Removes all non-data elements (axes, labels, grid). This is technically the most “advanced” use case as it is required for non-standard plots like maps, pie charts, or network diagrams.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_void()

















NoteConfiguration



A stable, bare-bones theme primarily used by developers for visual unit testing to ensure new features don’t break existing layouts.




Show the code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_test()
